title: CPython crashes on memory-allocation failure (OOM): 35 findings  (4 dups → 31 distinct)
author: devdanzin
body:
# Crash report

### What happened?

Allocation-failure fuzzing of CPython `main` turned up **35 distinct ways the interpreter
crashes** — segfault, failed `assert(...)`, or `Py_FatalError` — when a memory allocation (4 have since been confirmed as duplicates via `rr` → 31 distinct; see the dedup comment)
fails part-way through an operation. Each is a separate underlying bug with a minimal,
stdlib-only reproducer, a backtrace, a root-cause analysis, and a suggested fix, published
as a self-contained gist (linked below).

I'm filing them under one umbrella so they can be picked off individually without flooding
the tracker with 35 issues at once. **To take one:** open a normal CPython issue (or PR) for
it and drop a comment here with the link — I'll mark it in the table. If any turn out to be
duplicates or non-bugs, say so and I'll annotate them.

These were found with [fusil](https://github.com/devdanzin/fusil)'s OOM-injection mode
(fusil originally by @vstinner). The reports and reduced reproducers were drafted with
AI assistance (Claude Code) and then reviewed and re-verified by hand — see *Disclosure*.

## Reproducing

- Found on `main` (3.16.0a0) at commit `15d7406`. These are allocation-path bugs, so they
  are not tied to that exact revision.
- Every gist ships a minimal `OOM-ID-repro.py` (standard library only) that drives the failing path
  while `_testcapi.set_nomemory(...)` forces allocations to fail. Just run **`python
  OOM-ID-repro.py`** — where the exact failing-allocation index is sensitive to the build's
  allocation count, the repro *self-sweeps* (a fresh subprocess per index) and stops at the
  first crash, so no manual tuning is needed.
- `_testcapi.set_nomemory` requires a test/debug interpreter (`--with-pydebug` exposes it).
- **Build matrix** (in each report): many of these are debug-only assertion / `Py_FatalError`
  failures — the `assert(...)` is compiled out under `-DNDEBUG`, so a *release* build doesn't
  abort. They are still real bugs: on release the same defect is latent undefined behaviour
  (a use-after-free, a `Py_DECREF(NULL)`, or a silently-lost exception). A subset segfault on
  release builds directly; those are called out below.

## Highest-confidence starting points

These crash a **release** build (not just a debug assertion) **and** have a clean minimal
reproducer — the lowest-effort to confirm and fix:

- **OOM-0034**, **OOM-0028** — unchecked `PyUnicode_AsUTF8` / `PyUnicode_EncodeFSDefault`
  returning `NULL` is dereferenced (≈ one-line NULL checks).
- **OOM-0001**, **OOM-0002**, **OOM-0014** — `Py_DECREF(NULL)` on an unchecked-allocation
  error path.
- **OOM-0031** — `_interpreters.capture_exception` calls `_PyXI_FreeExcInfo(NULL)` with no
  NULL guard.
- **OOM-0012**, **OOM-0020** — instrumentation / thread-state corruption under OOM.

## Findings

Status: blank = not yet filed · `#N` = a CPython issue is open · 🔁 = **confirmed duplicate, folded into another finding — please don't pursue**.

### Segfaults (7)

| Report | Title | Status |
|---|---|---|
| [OOM-0001](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/464cef74ca8186843f33a38078476ac4) | Segfault: `Py_DECREF` of a NULL `filename` in `do_warn` (`_warnings.c:1139`) | [#151673](https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/151673) |
| [OOM-0002](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/2dfeabe7508f8e98f27f6df7e381f1cf) | Segfault: `Py_DECREF(NULL)` in `PyContextVar_Set` (`context.c:367`) | |
| [OOM-0024](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/acf15ad4117c6343b48ed8fdfe7ad167) | Segfault: dealloc of uninitialized iterator in `template_iter` (`templateobject.c:232`) |#151815 |
| [OOM-0028](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/774867b89b3de8d36d7e2ac405034577) | Segfault: NULL deref in `os__path_normpath_impl` (`posixmodule.c:6149`) | |
| [OOM-0031](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/44ffdf25538575e497fd80552ea5d467) | Segfault: NULL `info` deref in `_excinfo_clear_type` (`crossinterp.c:1319`) | #152013 |
|  [OOM-0033](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/249032e1746d63406a5f68d7dfdedb79) | [SUPERSEDED — duplicate of OOM-0036 / #151818] Segfault / negative-refcount: over-decreffed `sys.path` entry in `PyType_IsSubtype` (`typeobject.c:2931`) | 🔁 dup of OOM-0036 → [#151818](https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/151818) |
| [OOM-0034](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/9871a21facf4c9c6a415e220f9d10762) | Segfault: unchecked `PyUnicode_AsUTF8` NULL deref in `pegen.c:33` | #151798 |

### Assertion failures / aborts (23)

| Report | Title | Status |
|---|---|---|
| [OOM-0003](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/b628c59722820b55c61a400a973771d5) | Abort: uninitialized `_co_unique_id` assert in `code_dealloc` (`codeobject.c:2440`) | #152034 |
| [OOM-0004](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/315e83b2da6a5809ce5ae1d748cdd0ae) | Abort/Segfault: corrupted object freelist in `clear_freelist` (`object.c:909`) | |
|  [OOM-0005](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/22b71f61343c81df5bea9b7fca798e87) | [SUPERSEDED — duplicate of OOM-0036 / #151818] Abort: negative-refcount over-decref in `_PyFrame_ClearLocals` (`frame.c:101`) | 🔁 dup of OOM-0036 → [#151818](https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/151818) |
| [OOM-0006](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/c809eb4072c0c787c0c890f54ba1c843) | Abort/Segfault: `_PyObject_GC_UNTRACK` assert on untracked iterator in `dictiter_dealloc` (`dictobject.c:5532`) | |
| [OOM-0008](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/3c6690d0561acc62752a953e12b20197) | Abort: `assert(!PyErr_Occurred())` in `_PyType_LookupStackRefAndVersion` (`typeobject.c:6343`) | |
| [OOM-0009](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/34b633230f6d2301ba17dec195ffe4b7) | Abort: stale `release1` flag trips an ownership assert in `replace` (`unicodeobject.c:10783`) | #152228 |
| [OOM-0010](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/d3c1d06e95c006a320dbbfffcc210d52) | Abort: `assert(_PyErr_Occurred(tstate))` in `_PyEval_EvalFrameDefault` (`generated_cases.c.h:13817`) | |
| [OOM-0011](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/892b61619c1b3c8c2018331b3f1f4983) | [SUPERSEDED — duplicate of OOM-0008]  Abort: `assert(!PyErr_Occurred())` in `specialize` (`specialize.c:364`) | 🔁 dup of OOM-0008 (above) |
| [OOM-0012](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/610c147c8e2d42a576bab3c1c2713391) | Abort/Segfault: stale instrumentation in `get_tools_for_instruction` (`instrumentation.c:1106`) | |
| [OOM-0013](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/1736971107bea3aa04ce19db21c90845) | Abort: builtin breaks result/error contract in `_Py_BuiltinCallFastWithKeywords_StackRef` (`ceval.c:843`) | |
| [OOM-0014](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/ceb4b16662d22b3dcb1b56cd8481c9e7) | Abort/Segfault: unchecked NULL in `channelsmod__channel_id` (`_interpchannelsmodule.c:3487`) | |
| [OOM-0015](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/d40d39e6abfb746bd9d96de261205190) | Abort: stale exception in `cfunction_check_kwargs` (`methodobject.c:409`) | |
| [OOM-0016](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/05be8efe6b08c2b3ac3e2c17c784da0c) | Abort: `assert(!queue->alive)` in `_queue_clear` (`_interpqueuesmodule.c:559`) | #152130 |
| [OOM-0017](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/6e7a3a9487689e55d7dd4f70b1ce489f) | Abort: negative `gc_refs` ("refcount too small") in `validate_gc_objects` (`gc_free_threading.c:1116`) | |
| [OOM-0018](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/99b84915b197ae0ade7face262b8af66) | Abort: ownership assert in `set_keys` (`dictobject.c:205`) | #152254 |
| [OOM-0019](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/9bd9423256ea03c08231d1ebe542db89) | Abort: double-free in `_PyPegen_raise_error_known_location` (`pegen_errors.c:363`) | |
| [OOM-0025](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/3aaffc18b68ca2ae5fffa72aa6cdb2ea) | Abort: `assert(!PyErr_Occurred())` in `unspecialize` (`specialize.c:378`) | |
| [OOM-0026](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/bf6f784d4bcd21acd471ea45b6f23c70) | Abort: err-code vs `PyErr` desync in `handle_channel_error` (`_interpchannelsmodule.c:398` / `:443`) | #152156 |
| [OOM-0027](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/d5c602c29ac3881290269b444d77db3c) | Abort: `assert(PyStackRef_BoolCheck(cond))` in `POP_JUMP_IF_FALSE` (`generated_cases.c.h:11120`) | |
| [OOM-0029](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/10e0fdaf3d89dbe394d94fbf765c70a1) | [SUPERSEDED — duplicate of OOM-0036 / #151818] Abort: negative refcount on a `MemoryError` (`tuple_dealloc`, `tupleobject.c:277`) | 🔁 dup of OOM-0036 → [#151818](https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/151818) |
| [OOM-0030](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/fbfb9d6cd5eeb518e4f9eeab44be3893) | Abort: `Py_DECREF` of NULL-data unicode in `unicode_subtype_new` (`unicodeobject.c:13986`) | |
| [OOM-0032](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/f7e483080647c7b76fbda79bfeb07e9c) | Abort: pending-exception assert from `warn_explicit` normalization (`_warnings.c:799/806`) | |
| [OOM-0035](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/8c86ca358f3711740a692eaac730b527) | Abort / malformed str: invalid `maxchar` in `_PyUnicode_FromUCS4` (`unicodeobject.c:2228`) | |

### Fatal Python error (5)

| Report | Title | Status |
|---|---|---|
| [OOM-0007](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/bf9fec4554c58c2a279b05b7ff8e6d9b) | Fatal: `context_tp_dealloc` clears the pending exception (`context.c:535`) | #152083 |
| [OOM-0020](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/df523c79368baa0c44bd69e9d5ee0c7e) | Fatal: `_PyMem_DebugRawFree: bad ID` in `free_threadstate` (`pystate.c:1527`) | |
| [OOM-0021](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/e113c48a4e249ae7e4d1e42020db65c4) | Fatal: NULL returned without an exception set in `_Py_CheckFunctionResult` (`call.c:43`) | |
| [OOM-0022](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/0964a71a7038ee90137f11c2527aad3c) | Fatal: stale `MemoryError` trips `_Py_CheckSlotResult` in `reload_singlephase_extension` (`import.c:2011`) | |
| [OOM-0023](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/dc5123e50ea0402292e841411a294d3d) | Fatal: dealloc clears the in-flight exception in `subtype_dealloc` (`typeobject.c:2719`) | |

## Related groups (one fix may cover several)

- **Dealloc clears the in-flight exception** (the gh-89373 `_Py_Dealloc` invariant): **OOM-0007**
  (`context_tp_dealloc`) and **OOM-0023** (the generic `subtype_dealloc`, covering a family of
  pure-Python types) free an object while a `MemoryError` is pending and don't save/restore it.
- **A pending exception survives into code that asserts there is none** (specializer / eval /
  call layers): **OOM-0008, 0010, 0015, 0025, 0032** (OOM-0011 was a second detector face of
  OOM-0008 — same `frame.f_back` swallow — and is folded). Several share the theme that the
  adaptive specializer / call machinery is entered with a `MemoryError` already pending.
- **`Py_DECREF`/`Py_CLEAR` of a NULL or partially-initialized object on the OOM error path:**
  **OOM-0001, 0002, 0006, 0014, 0024, 0030, 0031** (an allocation fails after a slot is taken
  but before the object is valid, and the error path frees it anyway).
- **Over-decref → negative refcount under OOM** (a real memory-safety bug; the assert is the
  debug-build detector): **OOM-0019**. *(rr later showed OOM-0005, OOM-0029 and OOM-0033 are
  all the same `_CALL_LIST_APPEND` `list.append()`-under-`MemoryError` double-free —
  **OOM-0036, filed [#151818](https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/151818)** — and are
  folded; OOM-0019 is a distinct pegen double-free.)*
- **Free-threading-specific:** **OOM-0003** (`_co_unique_id`), **OOM-0017** (cyclic GC),
  **OOM-0018** (managed dict), **OOM-0020** (thread-state reservation).

## Disclosure & caveats

- The reports and reduced reproducers were **drafted with AI assistance (Claude Code)**; each
  gist carries an explicit disclaimer. The reproducers were re-run on the build matrix and the
  root causes audited against the CPython source before publishing.
- A few root causes are explicitly marked **partial** — the trigger is minimal and verified,
  but the exact offending line wasn't pinned (noted in those reports): OOM-0010, 0027, 0035.
- **OOM-0001** is already filed as
  [#151673](https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/151673); the other 34 had no matching
  python/cpython issue when checked and appear novel.

---

*Found with [fusil](https://github.com/devdanzin/fusil) (OOM-injection mode; fusil originally
by Victor Stinner). Drafted with Claude Code; reproducers machine-generated and human-verified.*


### CPython versions tested on:

CPython main branch

### Operating systems tested on:

Linux

### Output from running 'python -VV' on the command line:

Python 3.16.0a0 free-threading build (heads/main:15d74068f3a, Jun 18 2026, 16:44:30) [Clang 22.1.2 (1ubuntu1)]

<!-- gh-linked-prs -->

comments.author: zainnadeem786
comments.body:
Hi @devdanzin ,

I'd like to investigate and work on a couple of the findings from this umbrella issue.

I spent some time reviewing the relevant code paths and, based on source inspection, I'm currently looking at:

* **[OOM-0028](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/774867b89b3de8d36d7e2ac405034577)** (`os__path_normpath_impl` in `Modules/posixmodule.c`)
* **[OOM-0034](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/9871a21facf4c9c6a415e220f9d10762)** (`_PyPegen_byte_offset_to_character_offset_line` in `Parser/pegen.c`)

### OOM-0028

My investigation suggests that there is a potential OOM error path in `os__path_normpath_impl()` where `result` may become `NULL` after Unicode object creation fails, but execution can still proceed into the bytes-path encoding logic. This appears to be a localized fix with conventional `NULL` propagation semantics.

### OOM-0034

For OOM-0034, I found that `PyUnicode_AsUTF8()` may return `NULL` under allocation failure, while the returned pointer is later used without a guard. However, this one appears slightly more involved because the surrounding tokenization path may also need proper error propagation rather than only adding a local `NULL` check.

### Next Steps

My plan is:

1. Prepare a focused PR for **OOM-0028** with a minimal fix and regression coverage if feasible.
2. Investigate and prepare a separate PR for **OOM-0034**, ensuring that any `MemoryError` is propagated correctly through the caller chain rather than only preventing the immediate crash.

I'll continue investigating these code paths and will post updates here as I make progress. If the analysis holds up, I'll prepare focused PRs for the relevant fixes and link them in this issue.

Thanks for collecting and documenting these findings.

comments.author: zainnadeem786
comments.body:
I’d like to take [OOM-0014](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/ceb4b16662d22b3dcb1b56cd8481c9e7) if no one else is already working on it.

I reproduced it locally on both debug and release builds.

Summary of my investigation:

- `_interpchannels._channel_id(0)` calls `channelsmod__channel_id()`.
- `channelsmod__channel_id()` calls `get_module_from_owned_type(cls)`.
- That path can return `NULL` under OOM because `_get_current_module()` allocates the module name with `PyUnicode_FromString(MODULE_NAME_STR)`.
- `channelsmod__channel_id()` currently treats that return value as infallible:

```c
PyObject *mod = get_module_from_owned_type(cls);
assert(mod == self);
Py_DECREF(mod);
````

When the allocation fails:

* debug build aborts at `assert(mod == self)` because `mod == NULL`
* release build reaches `Py_DECREF(NULL)` and crashes with an access violation

The minimal fix appears to be a local NULL check:

```c
PyObject *mod = get_module_from_owned_type(cls);
if (mod == NULL) {
    return NULL;
}
assert(mod == self);
Py_DECREF(mod);
```

This preserves the existing invariant check and correctly propagates the pending `MemoryError`.

I also verified the focused test suite still passes locally:

```text
PCbuild/amd64/python_d.exe -m test test__interpchannels
Result: SUCCESS, 73 tests run, 5 skipped
```

I’ll prepare a focused PR with the NULL check and a small subprocess regression test unless there are concerns with this approach.

comments.author: manoj-marimuthu
comments.body: Working on [OOM-0019](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/9bd9423256ea03c08231d1ebe542db89), will open an issue and a pr soon

comments.author: devdanzin
comments.body:
**OOM‑0005 update**: the use‑after‑free is now confirmed (not just the debug assert).
When I filed this issue I could only confirm the negative‑refcount abort on the debug build and noted the release behaviour was a *latent* UAF I hadn't pinned. There's now direct evidence.

A reduced, stdlib‑only reproducer (`pkgutil.get_importer(s)` → `os.fsdecode` → `os.fspath`, with the string also kept alive in a dict) makes the `_PyFrame_ClearLocals` over‑decref free a still‑referenced object:

- On a debug GIL + ASan build, ASan reports a clean `heap-use-after-free` whose freed‑by stack is exactly this site — `PyStackRef_XCLOSE` (`pycore_stackref.h:726`) ← `_PyFrame_ClearLocals` (`frame.c:101`) during `exit_unwind`; the read is a later `Py_INCREF`, the allocation is the victim string. So it's not free‑threading‑specific and not assert‑only.
- On a debug free‑threaded build the same freed local is instead used by `PyOS_FSPath` (`Modules/posixmodule.c`) → `PyType_HasFeature` reading `Py_TYPE()` on freed memory → SIGSEGV.

So this is a genuine memory‑safety use‑after‑free reachable through ordinary stdlib, not merely a refcount‑debug assertion. Updated gist (adds `repro_uaf.py` + `backtrace_uaf.txt`): https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/22b71f61343c81df5bea9b7fca798e87

_(Investigation and draft assisted by Claude Code.)_

comments.author: tonghuaroot
comments.body: Opened #151968 for OOM-0013 — `compile()`, `exec()`, `eval()` and `ast.parse()` could return NULL without setting an exception when an allocation fails, crashing on the result/error contract assertion. The PR sets the error indicator at `new_compiler()`, the `_PyPegen_run_parser()` result/exception check, and the tokenizer-init helpers.

comments.author: zainnadeem786
comments.body:
Hi @devdanzin @StanFromIreland 

I'd like to investigate [OOM-0020](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/df523c79368baa0c44bd69e9d5ee0c7e) and [OOM-0030](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/fbfb9d6cd5eeb518e4f9eeab44be3893) if no one else is already working on them.

My plan is to first reproduce both crashes locally, trace the relevant ownership and error-propagation paths, and determine whether they can be addressed with focused fixes before preparing separate PRs.

I'll post updates here as I confirm the root causes and make progress.

Thanks!

comments.author: zainnadeem786
comments.body:
Hi @devdanzin,

I investigated **OOM-0020** and was able to reproduce it on current `main` in both free-threaded debug and release builds.

From tracing the allocation and cleanup paths, the crash appears to occur because the preallocated initial thread state can enter the cleanup path before its interpreter back-pointer has been initialized. As a result, `free_threadstate()` fails to recognize it as the embedded initial thread state and incorrectly calls `PyMem_RawFree()` on memory that is embedded within `PyInterpreterState`.

The fix appears to be localized, and I'm preparing a focused PR based on this investigation. I'll link it here once it's ready.

I'm also continuing to investigate OOM-0030 separately.

comments.author: zainnadeem786
comments.body:
Hi @devdanzin,

I investigated two additional findings from this umbrella issue and have opened focused PRs for both.

### OOM-0020

Opened: **GH-https://github.com/python/cpython/pull/152165**

This issue was caused by an early cleanup path in the free-threaded interpreter. During thread state allocation, `tstate->base.interp` was not initialized before later allocations could fail. If cleanup ran in that window, `free_threadstate()` could not recognize the embedded initial thread state and attempted to free embedded memory, leading to `_PyMem_DebugRawFree: bad ID` (debug) or an access violation (release).

The fix initializes the interpreter back-pointer immediately after obtaining the thread state, allowing the existing cleanup logic to correctly identify the embedded initial thread state while leaving the normal successful initialization path unchanged.

---

### OOM-0030

Opened: **GH-https://github.com/python/cpython/pull/152164**

This issue occurs during cleanup of a partially constructed non-compact Unicode subtype after an allocation failure. In debug builds, `unicode_is_singleton()` could attempt to read the first character from an object whose internal data pointer was still `NULL`, triggering the `data != NULL` assertion.

The fix adds a narrow guard for this temporary construction state before calling `PyUnicode_READ_CHAR()`. Fully initialized Unicode objects continue to follow the existing invariants, while failed construction now cleans up without triggering the debug assertion.

Both fixes were reproduced locally, validated after the changes, and submitted as separate focused PRs to keep review straightforward.

Thanks again for collecting and documenting these OOM findings—they've been very helpful to investigate.

comments.author: tonghuaroot
comments.body:
Taking OOM-0033. Reproduced on a current `--with-pydebug` `main` build — deterministic `_Py_NegativeRefcount` abort (the negative refcount surfaces in `typeobject.c` `is_subtype_with_mro` during exception/traceback construction).

Ablation root-causes the over-decref **producer** to the `zipimport` path hook (`zipimporter.__init__`) on its OOM error path: running each `sys.path_hooks` entry in isolation, `zipimporter` aborts while `FileFinder` is clean, and removing/replacing the zip hook eliminates the crash. It only triggers on the `_path_stat`-fails archive-search retry (an already-existing archive is clean), and only with heap-mortal single-character path components (latin-1 1-char strings are immortal, so the extra decref is a no-op there — which is why the reproducer needs a non-ASCII path element).

Pinning the exact unmatched `Py_DECREF` statement needs an `-O0` watchpoint (the optimized build inlines the decref call sites); I'm working on that and will follow up with a PR.

Reproduced and analysis prepared with AI assistance (Claude Code), reviewed and re-verified by hand. Finding credit is @devdanzin's (found with fusil OOM-injection fuzzing).

---

**Update (pinned with an `-O0` watchpoint):** the double-close is in `_CALL_LIST_APPEND` (`Python/bytecodes.c`) — its list-resize `MemoryError` path uses `ERROR_NO_POP()` after `arg` was already consumed by `_PyList_AppendTakeRef`, so the exception unwind `PyStackRef_XCLOSE`s it a second time (regression from GH-142711; the sibling ops `LIST_APPEND` / `SET_ADD` use `ERROR_IF`). I see this already has its own issue (gh-151818) and fixes in flight (#151826, #152114) — same root cause, so I'm **standing down** here, no PR needed. Thanks @devdanzin.

comments.author: devdanzin
comments.body:
> I see this already has its own issue ([gh-151818](https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/151818)) and fixes in flight ([#151826](https://github.com/python/cpython/pull/151826), [#152114](https://github.com/python/cpython/pull/152114)) — same root cause, so I'm **standing down** here, no PR needed.

Sorry for the trouble, I've just found out it's the same issue using rr :(
I'm going through each finding with rr to see if any others are duplicates.
Sorry again!

comments.author: devdanzin
comments.body:
**rr dedup pass — 4 of the 35 are confirmed duplicates (please don't pursue these)**

Following up on my note above: I went through the over-decref / negative-refcount and stale-`MemoryError` clusters with `rr` reverse-execution to pin each crash's *producer* (for these, the assert/segv site is a *detector*, not the bug). Four turned out to be duplicates, now folded:

| Finding | Actually | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| **OOM-0005** (`_PyFrame_ClearLocals` over-decref, `frame.c:101`) | **OOM-0036 → [#151818](https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/151818)** | rr ledger: the over-decref'd object is freed by `_PyList_AppendTakeRefListResize` ← `_CALL_LIST_APPEND` — the `list.append()`-under-`MemoryError` double-free. `frame.c:101` is just where the reused object is next touched. |
| **OOM-0029** (`tuple_dealloc` negref, `tupleobject.c:277`) | **OOM-0036 → #151818** | same double-freed appended item, surfaced at tuple teardown. |
| **OOM-0033** (`PyType_IsSubtype` over-decref of a `sys.path` entry) | **OOM-0036 → #151818** | same; `sys.path[:]=…; __import__` internally `list.append`s a second-referenced object under OOM. |
| **OOM-0011** (`specialize` `assert(!PyErr_Occurred())`, `specialize.c:364`) | **OOM-0008** | rr: the stale `MemoryError` is set by `PyFrame_GetBack` swallowing it while materializing `frame.f_back` — exactly OOM-0008, caught at a different `!PyErr_Occurred()` checkpoint. |

So **OOM-0036 (#151818)** is the canonical bug behind OOM-0005/0029/0033, and **OOM-0008** already covers OOM-0011. I've struck those rows in the table above, removed OOM-0033 from the starting-points list, and retitled the corresponding gists `[SUPERSEDED — duplicate of …]`. Thanks to everyone who looked at these — sorry for the wasted cycles.

(Method, for anyone curious: record the repro under `rr`, watch the victim's refcount / `tstate->current_exception`, and `reverse-continue` to the producing decref / exception-set. For the over-decref family it reliably lands on OOM-0036.)

_Dedup analysis and comment draft had assistance from Claude Code_

comments.author: Abhi210
comments.body: I will look into [OOM-0023](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/dc5123e50ea0402292e841411a294d3d)	Fatal: dealloc clears the in-flight exception in subtype_dealloc (typeobject.c:2719). Will open a PR for this soon.

comments.author: Abhi210
comments.body:
@devdanzin  I was looking into [OOM-0023](https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/dc5123e50ea0402292e841411a294d3d), and while implementing the proposed fix, I ran into another issue that appears to be distinct from OOM-0023. It looks like a separate free-threaded bug that becomes visible once this fix is applied, rather than being introduced by it.

The changes I made are:

```diff
 > diff --git a/Objects/typeobject.c b/Objects/typeobject.c
index d52cdedff10..3cf7c7afbd8 100644
--- a/Objects/typeobject.c
+++ b/Objects/typeobject.c
@@ -2833,6 +2833,13 @@ subtype_dealloc(PyObject *self)
        }
    }

+    /* Save the current exception: clear_slots() and the dict/base-dealloc
+         below decref arbitrary attribute values, which may clear
+           tstate->current_exception (gh-89373).  Mirroring slot_tp_finalize(). */
+    PyThreadState *tstate = _PyThreadState_GET();
+     PyObject *exc = _PyErr_GetRaisedException(tstate);   /* preserve in-flight exception */


    /*  Clear slots up to the nearest base with a different tp_dealloc */
    base = type;
    while ((basedealloc = base->tp_dealloc) == subtype_dealloc) {
@@ -2882,6 +2889,9 @@ subtype_dealloc(PyObject *self)
    if (type_needs_decref) {
        _Py_DECREF_TYPE(type);
    }

+    /* Restore the saved exception (see save above). */
+   _PyErr_SetRaisedException(tstate, exc);
}

static PyTypeObject *solid_base(PyTypeObject *type);
```

After making this change, I consistently hit the following assertion:

```

python: Objects/dictobject.c:207: set_keys: Assertion `_Py_IsOwnedByCurrentThread((PyObject *)mp) || IS_DICT_SHARED(mp)' failed.
Fatal Python error: Aborted


  Binary file "../cpython/python", at _Py_HandlePending+0x190 [0x56b5e10dde7a]
  Binary file "../cpython/python", at PyObject_Vectorcall+0x27 [0x56b5e0d4fde1]
  Binary file "../cpython/python", at _Py_VectorCall_StackRefSteal+0x11d [0x56b5e1006879]
  <truncated rest of calls>

Extension modules: _testcapi (total: 1)
[1]    465360 IOT instruction (core dumped)  ../cpython/python OOM-0023-repro.py

```

It seems to me that it aborts on (need to check it very carefully though, since I used Claude Code to debug this):

```
Objects/dictobject.c:207: set_keys:
  Assertion `_Py_IsOwnedByCurrentThread((PyObject *)mp) || IS_DICT_SHARED(mp)' failed.
```

Since this assertion originates from a different function/subsystem than OOM-0023, would you prefer that I fold this into the same PR, or track it as a separate issue? I'm happy to do either.

For reference, this was reproduced on the latest `main` branch.

comments.author: devdanzin
comments.body:
@Abhi210 Thanks for flagging this and sorry for the delay. It is a genuinely distinct, pre-existing bug that your OOM-0023 fix unmasks. Nice catch! I reproduced and root-caused it, here's what I found with help from Claude.

----

**Answer to your question: file it as a separate issue, don't fold it into the OOM-0023 PR.** Your exception save/restore is correct and can't touch dict ownership — it's a completely different subsystem, and keeping the PRs separate keeps both reviewable and bisectable.

**Root cause.** The assertion (`set_keys` → `ASSERT_OWNED_OR_SHARED`, `Objects/dictobject.c:205`) fires from the **`MemoryError`-recovery branch** of `PyObject_ClearManagedDict`:

```c
err = detach_dict_from_object(dict, obj);   // materializes the keys — this ALLOCATES
if (err) {                                   // reached ONLY on MemoryError
    assert(PyErr_Occurred() == PyExc_MemoryError);
    PyErr_FormatUnraisable("Exception ignored while clearing an object managed dict");
    set_keys(dict, Py_EMPTY_KEYS);           // Objects/dictobject.c:7896  ← the assert
    dictkeys_decref(oldkeys, IS_DICT_SHARED(dict));
    ...
```

When an object with a materialized managed dict (still pointing at its inline values) is reclaimed by the **cyclic GC** — i.e. it's part of a reference cycle — `delete_garbage → subtype_clear → PyObject_ClearManagedDict` tries to materialize the keys via `detach_dict_from_object()`, which **allocates and fails under OOM**. The recovery path then calls `set_keys(dict, Py_EMPTY_KEYS)`. But mid-collection the free-threaded GC has repurposed the dict's `ob_tid` to hold `gc_refs` (it restores `ob_tid` only for *reachable* objects — `gc_free_threading.c`), and a single-threaded program never marks a dict `_PyGC_BITS_SHARED` (`ensure_shared_on_read`/`ensure_shared_on_resize` only fire on cross-thread access). So `_Py_IsOwnedByCurrentThread(dict) || IS_DICT_SHARED(dict)` is false → abort.

That `set_keys` line is reachable **only** when the allocation fails, which is exactly why it needs OOM — a plain garbage-cycle `gc.collect()` doesn't hit it (the `detach` succeeds, `err == 0`).

**Minimal reproducer** — stdlib + `_testcapi` only, no argparse/http.server, and (importantly) **no OOM-0023 involvement**. Reproduces 8/8 on a stock free-threaded debug build (`--disable-gil`, `Py_DEBUG`):

```python
import gc
from _testcapi import set_nomemory, remove_mem_hooks

class C:
    pass

def make_cyclic():
    o = C()
    o.__dict__                       # materialize the managed dict (keeps inline values)
    o.a = 1; o.b = 2; o.c = 3
    o.self = o                       # self-cycle → only the cyclic GC can reclaim it

for start in range(300):
    make_cyclic()
    set_nomemory(start, start + 25)  # fail an allocation window during gc.collect()
    try:
        gc.collect()                 # delete_garbage → ClearManagedDict → detach fails → set_keys
    finally:
        remove_mem_hooks()
```

**Scope / severity.** Free-threaded **`Py_DEBUG` only**: on a release build the assert is compiled out and it's benign (the collection is stop-the-world and this is single-threaded, so the `set_keys` store is safe); on a GIL build the assertion doesn't exist. So it's a false/over-strict assertion on a legitimate OOM-recovery-during-GC path rather than a memory-safety bug — worth fixing, but lower severity than OOM-0023 itself.

**Likely fix direction** (for the free-threading / dict folks): the OOM-recovery `set_keys` / `dictkeys_decref` in `PyObject_ClearManagedDict` need to account for running during cyclic-GC clearing (where the dict's `ob_tid` is `gc_refs` scratch, not a thread id), or the assertion should exempt the stop-the-world-GC context.

----

Since you found it, it's yours to file. Feel free to use the repro and analysis above, and tag me on the issue if you'd like a review.

*Investigation and draft by Claude Code (Opus 4.8).*
